CURRENT AFFAIRR,

Mr. Slattery presided at the half-yearly meeting of
the “National *’ Bank one day last weeck. The ques-
tion of Irish on cheques cropped up, and Mr. Slattery
leathered out as if he were one of the famous “ mounted
fut.”” We are far from saying that there are not diffi-
culties in the way of banks in the matter of Irish sig.
natures, and there is no use shutting one’s eyes to.these
difficulties. When the Irish-speaking slave has had a
bit of spirit put into him, and when the Irish business
community have, as a body, a slight, but genuine,
regard for Irish, it will be easy enough to move the
banks. In the present state of affairs Mr. Slattery has
proved that Irish can bo flouted at a half-yearly meet-
ing of the “ National ”’ Bank, and this notwithstanding:

' that the modern hero of Fontenoy, Mr. R. Barry
O’Brien, was amongst the sharoholders present.

Dr. Henry, of London, who had given notico that he
would draw attention to the matter, was not present
at the meeting, so Mr. Slattery had the boards to him-
self, and did a war dance on the prostrate remains of
“Erse.””  We are not profoundly impressed by the
brains of the average bank clerk, though, as in the
ranks of all classes in Ireland, there are many wasting
their lives behind bank counters who are fit for better
things.  However, we do not share Mr. Slattery’s
pious horror at the suggestion that no clerk should be
promoted to the position of cashier unless he knew Irish,
if by the phrase “knowing Irish ”’ is understood know-
ing enough to be able to check a signature in the Irish
language as well as in English. Surely even the clerks
of the Provincial Bank would be equal to attaining to
that modest altitude of learning in a few months. Mr.
Slattery swelled to the occasion. The proposal that
members of the staff should not be promoted unless
they were qualified in Irish, filled him with
rhetoric. “It will be readily understood,”” said he,
“that if such a regulation were made and acted upon,
the effect would be to paralyse the business of the bank.”’
Well, the clerks of the “National’’ Bank must be
much bigger duffers than we think they are, if a pro-
posal to learn enough Irish to check a signature in Irish
would “ paralyse the business of the bank.”’” Of course,
that is all “ mounted fut *’ sort of talk on Mr. Slattery’s
part. y

This sort of intemperate anti-Irish harangue
naturally leads up to one of those bogus professions of
faith wath which we are so familiar in this raimess-
loaded country. ‘Now, ladies and gentlemen,” said
Mr. Slattery, “I yield to no man—and in this all my
colleagues are at one with me—I say I yield to no man
in the desire to promote and to foster the study of the
Irish language so as to gain, retain, and hand down to
future generations a knowledge of the rich early litera-
ture of our country in its archwmological and other in-
teresting branches; but I hold that, circumstanced as
wo are, it would be a grave mistake te attempt to force
on the employment of Irish in substitution for English
in banking transactions and commercial and financial
business, and so put a drag on their expansion.”” That
is the old familiar sort of thing! When Irish was a sort
of fossil movement, no doubt Mr. Slattery would laugh
at it; now that it is a real thing, and stretching its
limbs, Mr. Slattery “yields to no man’’ in his desire
to promote it as a fossil!

He trotted out O’'Connell and Lord Russell of Kil-
lowen as backers of his views against “ the unwisdom of
attempting to substitute Irish for English in matters of
commerce and business.”” Both men, however, were of
the last century of eclipse; this is the progressive twen-
tieth century, and even banks will have to march with
the times.

No shareholder present, note even Mr. R. Barmry
O’Brien, of famed Fontenoy, opened his mouth in
criticism of Mr. Slattery’s reactionary harangue, but a
Mr. H. W. Birks, of the London Stock Exchange,
whero, no doubt, so much of the money of the customers
of the “ National ’’ Bank is manipulated, rose and tilted
at “Erse;’” but yet “ Fontenoy, famed Fontenoy’’ in
the person of Mr. R. Barry O’Brien remained in a state
of being mute; now if he were conducting an excursion
party on tho battle-field not only fire would flash from
his eyes, but words of “burning eloquence ’’ would flow
I%Ke lava from his tongue. But on this occasion Mr.
Birks, of London, tilted at “Erse’’ without challenge. Mr.

Birks, of London, became facetious at the presumed
“very limited vocabulary’’ of “Erse.”’ He asked—“How
would a man ask a manager in Erse what was  the price
of a double option for a put and call?’ ”’ According to
the Freeman report the meeting indulged in laughter at
this burst of British wit. Might we point out to Mr.
Birks, of London, that the interrogative sentence he
quoted is not English at all, but Stock Exchange slang,
and that the sentence would convey no meaning what-
ever to, say, Addison or Macaulay, who probably had as
great a command of English as has even Mr. Birks of the
London Stock Exchange.

In an obscure corner of the Zndependent there are a
few paragraphs under the heading  Canals Inquiry in
Ireland.”” That is the sort of a heading that would
warn off many casual newspaper readers.  However,
the news matter underneath had roference to a meeting
of the Dublin Industrial Development Association.
Certainly the matter of the Commission on Canals
cropped up, but so also did a more interesting matter.
The last paragraph runs as follows:—“ No reply to a
letter from -the Council having been received from
Messrs. Humphrey, Stephen’s Green, a cheque for the
amount of the latter’s subscription was, the Secretary
intimated, drawn, and would be returned to them, and
their name would be removed from the list of members.””
The Independent was evidently afraid to boycott the
matter altogether. So it did the next thing: it hid it
away in an obscure corner under a heading “ Canals
Inquiry in Ireland.”” It is a significant fact that the
Dublin Industrial Association felt compelled to expel
from the association the British contractors of the Inter-
national Tmposition which is to be foisted on this country
- largely in the interest of the carrying companies.

Under the heading—“A Scotch Department in
Douglas ’’—a correspondent sends us some interesting
information concerning the woollen mills of Messrs.
Morrogh Bros. In the course of his letter our correspon-
dent says:— Few, if any, firmsin Munster have bene-
fited more by the LrapER’s campaign and the starting
of the Irish revival than Morrogh Bros.; nor do we
know of any firm that the Catholics of Cork—both lay
and clerical—have done more to encourage.”” The fol-
lowing facts will, we expect, come as a surprise to many
of our southern friends:—Our correspondent informs us
that the manager of the mills is Scotch. Of five tuners
five are Scotch, and two, at less pay than any of the
Scotchmen, are Irish; the designer is Scotch; the dyer
is Scotch, but the dyer’s assistant, a man or youth on
small pay, is Irish. The head warper is Scotch; the
finisher is Scotch, his assistant at about half the money
i3 Irish ; of the pattern weavers two are Scotch and six,
at less pay, are Irish. The only apprentice to the
designer is Scotch, so that in the designing line the
Scotch succession is preserved. The chances of local
hands reaching the leading positions do not appear to
be over bright. Recently two fresh Scotch familics
arrived. Is all this Scotch importation necessary, or is

< it partly a matter of one Scotchman bringing over
another, and the other bringing over two more, and so
on? Whilst Scotchmen are imported to Morroghs one
of the Morroghs was exported to the “awmy,” in
which he has a commission.

b Mr. Frederick Hoey, of Hoey and Co., Victoria
_ Street, Belfazt, a well-known wholesale spirit merchant,

Tho deposits of the Provincial Bank, one of the most
oxclusive corporations in Treland, were down by
£126,000 this half-year. So the chairman, a Mr, G.
D. Whatman, announced at the meeting of share-
holders. Evidently the meeting was a small one, and
Mr. John Gore was the only shareholder present to rub
in the salt about the diminished deposits. Said Mr.
Glore:—“ Tf there was any one sign more than another
of the public confidence in a bank it was the amount of
deposits. Was there any reason that the confidence of
tho public should decline with reference to the Provin-
cial Bank? They found it paid a dividend of 12 per
cent., which was a very fine dividend indeed, but
he said that they had ten directors on the Board, and he,
thought ho was right in saying that of those ten they
had five gentlemen who had done them the honour to
be present that day. In his view, mere ornamental
directors were more a source of loss to a bank than a
gain. The present feeling in this country with' regard
to a revival of Irish industries and Irish principles in
general might have something to do with the falling off
in the deposits, inasmuch as of the ten directors they
had very few Irishmen on the Board.”

It is to be regretted that no shareholders proposed
that open competitive examination for clerkships should
be introduced into this very much “Saved '’ concern.
However, we suppose the voice of Mr. Gore was the
first to let any sort of a “boo’’ out of it in the
presence of the directors and shareholders assembled of
that monument of “Saved’’ manipulation. Mr. Gore
broke the ice, and at the next half-yearly meeting we
may oxpect that the directors will 'be adequately
bearded in their dens. According to the disclosures
concerning the staffing of this bank that appeared in
the LEADER, there was no Papist on the general staff.
Of 54 managers, 52 were ““Saved!”’~of 83 tellers and
accountants, 75 were “ Saved,” and of 189 clerks, 170
were “Saved.”  Taking the staff in the lump, 302
were “Saved ” and a mere 29 were “ Idolators.”” No
wonder the deposits of this bank show a falling off.
But we are a long time waiting for the shareholders to
take action and demand that the clerkships be thrown
open to compatitive examination. With Mr. Gore lies
the honour of firing the first shot.

A reader in Blackrock makes an interesting sugges-
tion. Whilst Humphrey’s Dump is being pushed ahead
we hear little or nothing now of the National Exhibi-
tion. Our Blackrock reader wants to know if the pro-
posed boot factory could not be used as a counterblast
to Humphrey’s Dump. He asks if it would not be pos-
siblo to get ‘the National Exhibition people to co-operate
with the efforts to establish the boot factory, and hand
over the shares they have received to the labter.

. We do not know exactly how the National Exhibi-
tion project, sbfmd& But though tho suggestion of our
corrcspoudqnt is an interesting one, wo have our doubts
as to the wisdom of loading any business enterprise such
as the proposed boot factory with a contentious pro-
paganda. The boot factory had better come into the
world as an Trish boot factory, appealing to the public

and to the shops on the merits of its boots, and not in

any sense as an anti-Flumphrey’s Dump or anything of

that kind. However, it would be an excellont, thing it

(:.he boot factory were in full working order at the time
Ll;e‘ Humph_rey Imposition is foisted on the people.

Last year twelve students of St. Colman’s College,
Fermoy, matriculated in the Royal University ; this
year the number has increased to seventeen. The Col-
lege has to its credit four first class and two second class
honowrs, one first class and onesecond class exhibition.
In Dbrief, its record in connection with the R.U.I.
matriculation during 1906 is—Total distinctions, eight,
total passes, seventeon.

There is to be a Feis at Warrenpoint on 15th of
August, and we read in a local paper that it is being
organised by the Committee of the Warrenpoint Gacelic
League. Ts there a branch of the Gaelic League in the
place? We are informed that Warrenpoint cannot even
boast of an Irish class during the winter evenings.
There is an Amusements Committee there, and some
“God Save the King’ Pierrotts perform under its
auspices. We have reason to suspect that Mr. Bung
is not an uninterested party in relation to the forth-
coming Feis. Last year a Feis was held on the 15th
August, and Mr. Bung, we understand, had a good
deal to do with it, and his “takings’’ increased on

- that day. A local correspondent, in the course of a
letter to us, says :— If the Nationalists of Warrenpoint
(or the so-called ones) wanted a really Irish Fess, it
would be held on any other day but the 15th August,
but the Bung element of Warrenpoint thought of this

- all to attract a crowd of not Irishmen but flunkeys.”

The Sandyford Carnival and Féte will be opened on
Saturday next, August 4th, by the Lord Mayor. The

and wo learn that the football match on Monday
between the London Irish and Emmets is expected .to
attract a large crowd.

We offer our congratulations to our distinguished
contributor, 4n coatam Peaoap ua Laogaipe, or
his appointment to a Canonry in the Cathedral Chapter
of Cloyno. It will be diflicult to get used to the new
designation of tho great P.P. of Castlelyons. The
words, “.dan cdéan Pesopap,”  are household words
within the borders, and to a considerable extent outside
the borders also, of Irish Ireland; and henceforward
the author of Seaona is Canon O’Leary. It is difficult

A prospectus of the School of Medicine, Queen’s
College, Cork, is before us. Copies of it have been sent
to several priests in the County Clare, and if they were
sent to several it is reasonable to surmise that probably
they were sent to every priest within the radius of in-
fluence of the Queen’s College, Cork. What is the
object of sending such circulars to the “Idolatrous’”
clergy? 7They are not likely to go in for the study of
Medicine. Do the officials of the College wish to make
the clergy canvassers for pupils in that institution? If
so, do they know that the Queen’s Colleges, as well as
the College of the mud-throwers in College Green,
Dublin, are under the ban of the Hierarchy? A
reverend correspondent, in the course of a letter to us
on this matter, writes :—* The prohibition is to be found
in the ¢ Statutes of Maynooth Synod,” page 139. There
the bishops condemn these ¢ Godless Colleges,” and re-
solve to keep Catholics from entering them as far as
they can. Priests are forbidden under pain of suspen-
sion, ipso facto, to take office in these reprobated insti-
tutions. They are, moreover, forbidden under pain of
mortal sin to advise parents to send their children to
the aforesaid Colleges. If the authorities of the Cork
Queen’s Collego know these facts their sending such
circulars to the Catholic clergy, above all men in the
world, displays an amazing amount of ‘ cheek.””

The selection of the Hill of Uisneach as the object of
“Turas an Oireachtais’ for the present year is an
appropriate one on the part of the Dublin Coiste Ceann-
tair. The excursion to Tara last year proved a most
enjoyable conclusion to Oireachtas Week, and it is fit-
ting that it should be held on the present occasion on
the hill which was second to Tara alone in ancient Ire
land as a place for national assemblies. Its legendary
associations reach back to long before the dawn of his-
tory, and their pagan complexion is balanced by the
close relationship between Uisneach and our early saints,
and its connection with the early history of Irish Chris-
tianity. Tho excursion train leaves the Broadstone at
10.30 a.m. on Sunday, 12th August, the fare—which
includes admission to an acridheacht—being two

shilllings.

- Intending agricultural students should take note of
- the advantages offered by the well-known Irish College
of the Franciscan Brothers, Mount Bellew, Co. Galway.
- Their Agricultural College is situated in the grounds
of the TIntermediate College, and is conducted by
members of the community holding diplomas in
Ppractical science, technical agriculture and practical
~ agriculture. Laboratories, with all up-to-date require-
ments, together with a model farmyard, have beon
lately erected and equipped in it by the Department of
Agriculture.  In the farm gardens and laboratories
ample facilities are afforded for a thorough course in
technical agriculture as well as in the various other sub-:
jects set out in the syllabus. Theory is very littlo use
without practice, but there is no fear that the students
taught by the Franciscan Brothors will bo let off with a
mere theoretical knowledge. Wo note that the College
prospectus emphasises this point, as we read in it that
* The students are required to take pant in the work of
the fields, and of the farmyard and dairy.” We are
glad to add that Irish-manufactured machinery is ex-
clusively used at Mount Bellew Agricultural College.

THE EMIGRATION QUESTION.

Dear Sir,—In your issue of the 16th June, I brought
under the notice of your readers a practical case on the
emigration question, in the hope of eliciting some useful
suggestions that could be used with effect in dissuading
our people from emigrating. With the exception of
your own comments on the case, no one has yet at-
tempted to give any advice on the point at issue.

Now my contention is, that you cannot stop emigra-
tion on a sudden. The only cure for it is the creation
of remunerative work, that is, when the labourer can
command as good a price, comparatively speaking, for
his labour here at home as in America or elsewhere;
then and only then will emigration cease. The crea-
tion of labour to a great extent depends on the edu-
cated and monied class, so that if they have not the
interests and welfare of the country at heart, and are
not alive to the blighting effects of emigration, then it
would seem that there is no remedy for the evil. As
long as there is work and high wages obtainable in
America, so long will our people, who may have en-
dured untold hardships and misery at home, be tempted
to emigrate. There may be somo person from their
neighbourhood doing well in America; so being elated
by his success, and having the desire of bettering their

THE LEADER.

AUGUST 4, 1906,

present sad condition, they are dqtormjned to try their
lot in the land of freedom. Thero is no denying the fact
that tho most of those who have emigrated have suc-
ceeded well in America, and if it were not for the
money tliey sont home, thier families would have been
ovicted from thier little homes. True, many have not
succeeded, but then these would likewise have come to
the wall had they remained at home. _Emigra,tion was
caused by landlordism and the suppression of our indus-
tries, so in proportion as these causes are removed, emi-
gration will cease.

To return to your comments on the case, you ask:
“ Aro they sure that any bofter prospects await them
in America?’ Now that is the question they are goiag
to decide, and a great deal depends on circumstances.
As a matter of fact, T have learnt that the young men
in question have got employment at water-works at
wages of £9 por month. They had the option of several
jobs, so that there seems to be no scarcity of work. In
a letter home, they invited out their former companions,
saying that “ they wondered why they stayed in Ireland
when they could get such high wages in America.’’
Again, you ask what are their chances that they will
possess land in America? Now no man can get land,
unless he has the wherewith to purchase it; and at the
rate of wages given above, their chances of getting land
aroe good, if they only wish to save money to procure
it. They can return to Ireland after a number of years
and buy land, as many have done before them, so that
their hopes are not so gloomy as you would paint them.
At the rate of wages given, they can, if they wish, lay
something by for the “rainy” day, which they could
never do in Ireland at the rate of wages given for
labour.

In the present state of affairs in Ireland, emigration
is a necessary evil, and it will take many years to cure it,
as I do not see much improvement in the poorer and
more populous districts from which the people must
emigrate if they wish to gain a livelihood.—Yours faith-
fully, A NORTHERN READER.

SMUZTY PLAY.

¥~ HE “truly Catholic’’ Freeman of Monday pre-
! sonts contrasts that, unfortunately, we must °
admit are not uncharacteristic. On the leading page
thero was a. ““ pastoral kopje’’ leading article on “ The
University Question,”” and an advertisement of several
forthcoming plays, including “ The Spring Chicken! ’’
On page five, the principal news page, there appears
an interview with his Grace the Archbishop of Dublin,
in columns four and five, and in column six we read:—
“There will be return visits of the ‘ Duchess of Dant-
zic,” ‘The Spring Chicken,” ‘ The Orchid,” and ‘The
Little Michus,” all of which scored hugely on their pre-
vious visit, and come back to receive a warm welcome, to
make new friends, and with added honours fresh upon
them.’”” The italics are ours. We know nothing of any
of the “plays’’ except the “Spring Chicken,” and if
the puffing of that indecency is not “simply deplor-
able,”” we do not know the meaning of the phrase. Is
it by accident or design that the puff of this disgusting
piece was placed on the very page in which the interview
with his Grace the Archbishop appears? Showing up
the Freeman for puffing and advertising dirty plays
appears to be almost as barren as protesting against the
smutoscopes at the Royal Dublin Society’s Shows.

In our issue of February 24th, we published an
article on this detestable piece. The article was headed
‘¢ Putrid Filth ’ at the Dublin ‘Gaiety.”’”’ The phrase
“ Putrid Filth ’ was not ours; it was Mr. Stead’s. Our
article, indeed, was made up to a considerable extent of
extracts from an article which Mr. Stead wrote in his
Review of Reviews of this “putrid filth,”” which the
“truly Catholic’’ Freeman of Monday last, puffs on the
same page as it prints the interview of its representative
with his Grace the Archbishop of Dublin. ~We went
to the play, and in the course of our article on it we
said :—

THE AND K

Wo are not going to say much oursclves about this ““comedy.”

‘We wrote protty plainly in the past about ““Zaza,” “Kitty Grey,”
“Sapho,” and such abominations. We prefer to let Mr. Stead,
of the Review of Revicws, have his say on “The Spring Chicken.”
Mr. Stead being an Englishman, his opinion may have more
weight with the directors of the Gaiety Theatro and with a certain
non-Irish as woll as nondesoript portion of the public than we
mere Irish people might be calculated to have.

Mr. Stead headed his article on the play—puffed in
Monday’s Freeman on the same page on which is pub-
lished the interview with his Grace the Archbishop—
“ Gomorrah at the Gaiety ’—the “ Gaiety '’ referred to
by Mx. Stead was the London “ Gaiety,’’ not the imita-
tion one in South King Street. Mr. Stead wrote of it:—

As I camo out I oould not help recalling tho ghastly jest of
Mr. Punch, who represented ono poor, wretched, draggle-tailed
street walker accosting another as forlorn with the question, ‘“How
long have you been gay?”  Tor the gaiety of tho Gaiety Theatre
is as tlm”gnym:m of tho gay women on the streets, as hollow and
as base.

And this “gay ”’ play, months after it was condemaed
in such scathing terms by Mr. Stead, months after we
wrote of it in our issue of February 24th, is puffed by

374
‘the “truly Catholic”’ Freeman, and on the same day,
on the very same page, that an interview with his
Grace the Archbishop of Dublin appears. Did the
conductors of the Freeman intend this puff of what Mr.
Stead called “Gomorrah at the Gaiety’” as an
insult to his Grace the Axrchbishop of Dublin, or are
they so dead to logic, to propriety, to common decency

- ‘as to put the two items cheek by jowl, as it were, with-
out being conscious of the insulting contrast?

Lot us quote again from Mr. Stead :—
The whole. musical comedy is one long presentation of lust, un-

redeemed by a single spark of sent'ment. The wholo thing is
reduced to the level of monkeys at the Zoo.

Again, Mr. Stead says of onc of the characters in
the ¢ putrid filth *’ puffed in Monday’s “ truly Catholic’’
Freeman :—“ He locks himself into his office with frail
clients, and accompanies them to restaurants of ill-
fame.”” Mr. Stead also said:—

We have heard a good deal of the o dramatists of the
Restoration. But I doubt whether Wycherly or Congreve ever
compressed into any of their comedies a more compact mass of
dircy allusions and adulterous suggestions than those which pretty

© young girls make on the Gaicty stage for the edification of the

British public.

Mr. Stead further referred to “this pestilent and
pestiferous farrago of filth,”” and also, “The whole
thing is evil to the last degree. Everyone is pawing
with vice, hinting at it, grinning at it, indulging in it.”

Is there to be no end to this advertising and pufling
of filthy plays on the part of the *truly Catholic
Freeman?  What do the sharecholders think of \it?
Amongst the shareholders there must be a large number
who would be acutely shocked at the bare idea of their
dividends being contributed to by the puffing and ad-
vertising of “putrid filth;’’ will some of them kindly,
and in the interests of public morals and decency, raise
the question at the next meeting of shareholders of the
Freeman Co.?

Our readers will remember that when “ Sapho *’ went
to Limerick, his Lordship Bishop O’Dwyer took up a
bold stand, and yet the “truly Catholic’’ Freeman
actually puffs “ putrid filth,”” and not only puffs it but
puffs it in the same issue and on the same page as it
publishes an interview with his Grace the Archbishop
of Dublin and Primate of Ireland!

In Monday’s Freeman also there is a descriptive ac-
count of the great annual Pilgrimage to the summit of
Croagh Patrick, on which the Holy Sacrifice of the
Mass was offered, and where Father Forde delivered a
sermon in Irish. One could take extracts from the
report of the Pilgrimage that, if placed against the
puffs of “ putrid filth ** that appear in another column
of the same issue of the Freeman’s Journal, would
hurt the consciences of even the most careless or
hardened Catholic shareholders of the I'recman.

His Grace the Archbishop has already spoken against
smutty plays and smutty papers, and yet the Freeman
prints an interview with his Grace on the same page as
a puff of a vile and smutty play, and prints a “Kopjo
climbing ’ leading article in the same page on which
it advertises a smutty play. It is needless for us to
record our view that if his Grace had known that a
puff and an advertisement of a vile and smutty play
were to appear in the issue in which his interview with
the representative of the smut puffer of Princes Street
appeared, he would not have granted the interview.

Ever since our first appearance, now nearly six years
ago, when we discovered the Z'reeman and the Irish
Zwmes puffing a smutty music hall—mow, happily, dead
—we have heen attacking the smutty stage, and the
“truly Catholic’’ Freeman has been advertising it. Is
there to be no end to this sort of thing? If the Free-
man’s shareholders’ consciences are so tough that puffs
of “putrid filth > do not affect them when they cat the
bread purchased by Zreceman dividends, perhaps the
decent public of Dublin will rise in protest, not only
against the puffs of “putrid filth” in the Freeman,
but against its enactment on the boards of the Gadety or

any other theatre in the Catholic parts of Ireland.

up. of an Irish religious celebration in Dublin is nob

anted was an Irish sermon, I daresay thero would bo

submit, try to get up, if not this year, then next, some
ambitious Irish service of which such sermon would b.q
meroly a part. What form such service should take is
a maiter for discussion, and, taking it for granted that
there will ‘be such discussion amongst the delegates next
weoek, I tako the liberty of jotting down a point or two
that may help them to do their thinking. I amj of
course, speaking only of Catholic services. 5

The most ambitious Irish religious celebrations we
have had so far have taken the form of evening services.
Thoso held in London and that held this year in Belfast -
took that form, and were such a service possible in con-
nection with, or rather during the Oireachtas, we can
imagine how impressive it would be. Dubliners them-
solves best know whether or not it would be possible to
hold such an evening celebration; there may be ecir-
cumstances that put the organising of the like out of
the question. If, however, there be no such circum-
stances, I see hardly any other difficulty in the way.
The book of hymns, music, ete., would, I suppose, be
gladly given by the London or Belfast Committee, and
all the Dublin people would have to do would be to
provide the choir, and teach as big a congregation as
possible the hymns.

Failing an evening service, the next most obvious way
of proceeding would be to have High Mass with Irish
sermon, and, if such be permissible, choir or congrega-
tional accompaniment in Irish. Such a celebration
would be of sublime appeal, and I leave it to the
imagination of next week’s delegates what it micht be
made if a good number of the country clergy coming to
Dublin for the Oireachtas could be in town to take part
in it.

One more idea of what the celebration might be. T
have so far considered only Dublin, but—and especially
if the country clergy cannot be in Dublin for any service
that may bo there—why not each of those priests have
an Irish service in his own parish before he leaves to
take part in Ireland’s Oireachtas? It is a grand thing
to have Ireland recommencing to honour St. Patrick,
but, as far as the Gaelic League is concerned, the fact
is already almost forgotten in many districts that it
was the League started the agitation for an Irish and
a sober Patrick’s Day. Far be it from me to suggest
that we should organise religious celebrations for pro-
pagandist purposes. I would not, but, as I said in the
articlo referred to, whether it have propagandist effects
or not, I think it is time we openly besought God’s
blessing on our work. The Dublin celebration. with the
massed delegates in attendance, would be the ideal way
to do that. Failing such attendance the more celebra~
tions that could be held throughout the country the
better.

In conclusion, as I have no hope that there will be
any Irish religious celebration in Dublin next week,
might I suggest that one of the papers at next year’s
Maynooth Union should be on “Irish in Religious Ser-
vices?’ It is very evident that we all might know
more about the proprieties and the possibilities of the
matter than we do. We might also do much more than
we do.

HE publication of Part VIII. of the Religious
Songs of Connacht completes the second volume,
and the whole series, of that division of Dr. Hyde’s
collection of Connacht Songs. We have already con-
sidered the first volume, and now take up the second,
which is contained in Parts V. to VITI. This is the
only way to get an idea of the book as a whole, for the
sub-divisions have been made without reference to the
matter—one part ending and another beginning in the
middle, not only of a piece, but often of a sentence.
Like beads on a string, the parts are obviously not in-
tended to be viewed separately. Even the division into
volumes ds necessary only for convenient holding, the
two making an uninterrupted flow of peanacap aboub
the poems and stories which are introduced, giving
their history (when known) and the circumstances in
which they were obtained.
The pieces in Vol. II. are of much the same character
as those in Vol. I., consisting of prayers, hymns and
- charms in verse; and dialogues and stories in prose.
St,nictl‘y speaking, the prayers are, for the most part,
half “Verse, half prose, sometimes approaching to the
nature of blank verse, though the lines are shont; but
some of them are in regular metres. We have noted
before the spontaneity of these prayers. They are
beautifully simple and direct, and many of them touch
g immediately upon every-day life, showing—if that were
needed—how the Gael “feels God ever near him.”
Prayer is the first word with him, and the second, and
the last. Prayer on rising in.the morning; prayer on
making- a bed; prayer before meat and after;
prayer after tobacco; prayer before ‘going a
P journey; prayer on entering a church; prayer
after Communion; prayer on leaying a church;
prayer for health; prayer against various diseases;
prayer for a holy death; prayer for the dead; prayer
against the good people; prayer on milking a
cow ; prayer on making bread; prayer on covening the
fire; prayer on lying down to sleep. Instances of all
* these are to be found in Dr. Hyde’s book, yet we re-
g member only one case in which he says that the same
~ prayer is used by the people in English. So true is
it that the prayers will live or die as the Irish lives or
dies, for the spirit of such prayer is not in the English
tongue. 5 :
Among the longer prayers, dn Cpaoibin gives
~various forms of the well-known ““Opta fhurpe,” in-
~ cluding somo Highland versions, which are very in-
resting. The little ““ dipting Muipe,” too, 18 given
in a variety of ways, being known in many parts of tho
“Seatt Subdilcrve na Magome” is a
6 piece with a rann or refrain, which An Ciaoibin
ot from Dr. Conor Maguire and from others in County
. We have secen an old English Christmas Carol
The Seven Joys of Mary,”” which resembled this
so much that it would be interesting to
1y of both and to compare their dates.

Some of the prose stories aro interesting, and some
are amusing, such as “Seanacar ap Sotam,” in
which the half-fool gets the betier of Solomon, in spite
of all his wisdom. ~Another story gives a traditional
account; of tho origin of the Cross of Cong. Another is
a discussion on religion between two old women, Maipe
and Sigie, Sigle standing up for faith, and Mame

‘We often hear it said that Irish is “ a fine language

GAELIC FOQTBALLZAT JONES’S ROAD.

HE final of the Senior Football League between the
Geraldines and Kickhams took place on Sunday
last at Jones’s Road, and, needless to say, it started the
usual forty-five minutes late.  About two thousand
people made their way over the paling into the grounds
without payment. No attempt was made to keep them
out, and it is obvious that this was a matter of gross
neglect, while it emphasises the necessity of the Associa-
tion purchasing a ground of its own, and properly
enclosing it. S

I shall now endeavour to place before the readers of
the LEADER a fair and unbiassed account of how the
match was conducted. The clubs engaged were the best
two in Dublin, and it might be expected that those who
paid their money would be treated to a good dis-
play of football. If they went to Jones’s Road with
that idea they were sadly disappointed. I have seldom
seen a worse display. Fouls were so numerous that
good play was out of the question. The match may be
judged when I say that in fifty minutes there were
thirty-six “frees’’ distributed between the two teams.
One would expect that a match between two such teams
would pass off without trouble, but in that there was
further disappointment.

A rogrottablo incident occurred about eight minutes
from the end of the match, when the Kickhams, who
had just scored, were leading by 5 points to 4. The
captain of the Geraldines, who is a splendid, and to my
mind a very fair player, was rushing to take the ball,
but being a bit “late,”” the result was that he tripped
a Kickham player, both falling to the ground. The
roferoo ordered the captain of the Geraldines off the
field. After the usual protest he was walking off, but
pressure was brought to bear on him by those on the
side line, one of whom is an officer of the Association.:
Tho captain, however, eventually walked off as he was.
ordered. Two other Geraldine players preceded him,
and they deserve an enforced rest.  Eventually th

' whole team, including the Kickhams, walked off, as tho
match could not be proceeded with as matters stood.

Now I could not see why the Geraldine captain was
ordered off, as ho was about as fair a player as any on
the field as well as being one of the best, but that is no
excuse for any ‘other members of the team walking off.
‘The referee, in the opinion of good Jjudges of the game,
Was not a success. But even admitting that, the
players who walked off, or those who stopped the match,
cannot bo excused. If tho reforce be considered over so
unfair his rulings should be obeyed. Thero ought to bo
some more satisfactory means of protesting against a
biassed or inefficient referce than walking off the field of
play before the conclusion of a match. The governing
I.mdy should take steps to have a neutral refereo for
important matches such as that under notico, or at
least one who is capable of giving satisfaction.

It is only a couple of weeks ago since a club was
struck off the books of the Association, and fifteen out
of seventeen players of the team suspended or warned
off for gross misconduct on the field of play. Such
occurrences as these are a disgrace to the Association, and
if prompt steps are not taken to apply some drastic
remedy, it will be ruined. People are tired of going to
seo incompetent referees, free fights, or unseemly
wrangling on the playing pitch, as they pay their money
to see a match fought out to the end in a spirit of manly
rivalry. The affiliated clubs have the matter in their
own hands, but they are either unable or unwilling to
break up the Mutual Admiration Society, which runs
the Association at present. It is vain to hope for im-
- provement when those who help to frame the rules
incite others to break them.

A sailing regatta is one in which' mysterious boats
called yachts go cruising around as if they were going
out for mackerel.. To enjoy this kind of regatta you
need patience and a tolescope, and a belief that you aro
a yachtsman.

All regular regattas are always held on the wettest
daAy~ in the weok. They always (say they will) have
military bands, and “a grand display of fireworks in
the evening.”” But don’t believe them, Nora.

. Our cousin, Marie Josephine (who has been “finished’’

-In Belgium) wants to know what do Wolff and Holbein
want to swim the Channel for, when they could go
across twice as quick in the Dover-Calais steamer.

Now that we think of it, perhaps the Channel
swimmers have fallen back on natation as tho best way
to escape sea-sickness? Wo must try that ourselves
next time.

“ A reception ”’ was given to Mr. W. J. Bryan, of

speaker discovered that Mr. Bryan has Irish blood in
his veins—a most unusual place for blood to be. The
number of distinguished parties who have Irish blood

blood in their veins don’t seem to be thought much of.

“ there is not a day the sun sets in the Western Ocean
that our people don’t bless the name of America.”
Now, John, that’s just’ what wo may call flummery!

that would like to see the ’Murrican that would look
sour at you at such a momentous juncture in his
country’s history.

The papers do not say what was the effect in general
of the reception on the distinguished American, but we
aro told that when it was over he left immediately for
the Continent. But did he take all the flummery with
him? Puck.
